title: Foundations of Practical Quantum Advantage in Quantum-Informed Machine Learning for Predicting Chaos
authors: Maida Wang, Xiao Xue, Minh Chung, Peter V. Coveney
abstract: We develop theoretical foundations for a practical quantum-advantage mechanism in quantum-informed machine learning for chaotic dynamical systems. A family of k-indexed higher-order quantum statistical priors (Q-Priors) hosts the k-point marginal of the invariant measure on n_q = kq qubits, extending the single-site construction of prior work. We prove a two-stage advantage. In the representation stage, superposition and entanglement compactly store non-factorisable spatial correlations of the invariant measure on n_q qubits. In the extraction stage, joint Bell measurements on two copies estimate any post hoc Pauli functional with a copy-pair count independent of n_q, whereas any adaptive single-copy protocol for the corresponding full-Pauli read-out requires Omega(2^(n_q)) copies; this is a provable quantum-classical separation in copy-measurement complexity. The two-copy read-out is realised in simulation and on IQM superconducting processors. Two case studies instantiate the mechanism in workflows of independent scientific value: a turbulent channel-flow study in which the two-copy read-out yields a named non-diagonal correlator of the invariant measure (the velocity-direction coherence), and a medium-range weather forecasting workflow on the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts ERA5 reanalysis in which the diagonal k <= 2 Q-Prior steers a Koopman rollout, improves anomaly-correlation skill by 10-39% across 48-240 h lead times, and reduces the long-horizon collapse of rollouts onto a static mean field. The two conditions of our practical-advantage definition are met at complementary levels, identifying a candidate route to practical quantum advantage before fault-tolerant hardware.
summary: The paper proposes using Quantum-Informed Machine Learning (QIML) to improve forecasts of chaotic systems by encoding system statistics into a compact quantum prior. It proves a significant computational speedup by showing that joint Bell measurements on two copies can efficiently read out complex correlations, an advantage demonstrated in both fluid dynamics and weather forecasting simulations.
main_problem: Developing a theoretically grounded, practical quantum advantage mechanism within Quantum-Informed Machine Learning (QIML) for predicting complex chaotic dynamical systems.
main_result: The authors prove a two-stage advantage: using superposition/entanglement to compactly store non-factorizable correlations in a Q-Prior, and achieving an exponentially faster read-out of Pauli statistics via joint Bell measurements compared to classical single-copy protocols.
method: They introduce k-indexed higher-order quantum statistical priors (Q-Priors) that encode the invariant measure's marginals. This is coupled with a classical Koopman rollout, constrained by the quantum prior information.
model_or_system: The framework applies to chaotic systems like turbulent channel flow and medium-range weather forecasting using ERA5 data. The core computational element involves encoding system statistics into a quantum state on $n_q$ qubits.
key_observables: Velocity-direction coherence (non-diagonal correlator in turbulence); Anomaly Correlation Coefficient (ACC) improvement of 10-39% in weather forecasting; provable separation in measurement copy complexity ($M_Q$ vs $M_C$).
important_parameters: Number of qubits ($n_q=kq$), correlation order ($k$), and the required fidelity ($\eta, \delta$) for quantum advantage demonstration.
main_assumptions: The existence of a well-defined invariant measure for the chaotic system; the ability to perform two-copy Bell measurements on superconducting hardware (IQM).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between quantum and classical copy costs, showing the exponential separation. One figure compares QIML results across different baselines for turbulent flow, highlighting directional coherence recovery.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical foundation by defining the two-stage advantage (Representation $ightarrow$ Extraction). It then validates this mechanism through two distinct case studies: turbulence (demonstrating non-diagonal correlation read-out) and ERA5 weather forecasting (improving long-term prediction skill via diagonal priors).
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the interface between quantum computation and classical scientific modeling, providing a concrete, near-term pathway to demonstrating quantum advantage in complex physical simulations before fault-tolerant hardware is available.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two-stage quantum advantage architecture for QIML. Stage 1 is representation: a compact quantum register stores the Q-Prior (Appendix A, generalising [22]), whereas an explicit classical register or probability table loses the compression advantage. Stage 2 is extraction/read-out: Bell measurements on two copies of ρ(k) θ estimate post hoc Pauli statistics with MQ independent of nq at fixed accuracy and confidence. Adaptive single-copy read-out for the corresponding full Pauli task requires MC = Ω(2kq). The output is the numerical Q-Prior statistic used by the downstream classical model. The entire architecture is thus implemented as QIML [22].

FIG. 2. Post hoc Bell read-out of trained Q-Priors in simulation and on superconducting hardware. (A) Cryostat and qubit- connectivity maps of the two IQM Quantum Computers superconducting processors used: 20-qubit Garnet (nq ≤10) and 54-qubit Emerald (nq = 12–16); highlighted sites host the 2nq-qubit two-copy register. (B) Stage-2 extraction for the ERA5- trained Q-Prior in noiseless simulation: Bell estimates against exact |tr(Pρ)| for 22 diagonal and non-diagonal Paulis at a single nq, evaluated post hoc from a single Bell record of 1.2 × 105 copy pairs; shaded band ±η = 0.2 (distinct from the per-nq 8-observable test set tabulated in Appendix D). (C) Stage-1 representation for the prior...

FIG. 3. QIML on turbulent channel flow, a chaotic system that jointly closes Definition 1. (A) Velocity-direction phase field θ = arg(vx+ivy) of a representative snapshot (wall-normal y versus streamwise x), the intrinsic phase of the vector field, encoded one site per qubit. (B) Recurrent rollout in Lyapunov-time units (one step = 0.01 Lyapunov time): the ground truth and the QIML (Koopman with k ≤2 Q-Prior) prediction preserve the turbulent structure, whereas the Koopman-only, FNO and MNO baselines drift or collapse. The ground-truth and the Koopman/FNO/MNO baseline rollouts are from [22]; only the QIML rollout is computed here. (C) Directional coherence C(r) = |⟨ei(θA−θB)⟩| versus...

FIG. 4. QIML applied to weather forecasting. Panel A shows the QIML weather pipeline. Solid links represent the single-site and pairwise k ≤2 priors instantiated in the reported case study, connecting illustrative teleconnection nodes (e.g., PNA, NAO, MJO, IOD, SOI). Dashed higher-order links indicate k ≥3 Q-Prior extensions whose read-out tasks are covered by the theoretical results of this work. A parametrised quantum generator on nq = kq qubits is trained from samples so its induced low-order Q-Prior statistics match the data; the extracted Q-Prior statistics enter the Koopman autoregressive model through a differentiable statistical constraint.
